name: Totaka
dates_notes: One of the four chief disciples per tradition; the Toṭakāṣṭaka survives.
core_thesis: Toṭaka is best known through the *Toṭakāṣṭaka* — eight verses of *guru-stuti* in *toṭaka* metre (whence his received name) addressed to Śaṅkara as *Bhagavatpāda*. The hymn is doctrinally compressed and expresses the Advaita *guru-bhakti* register in its purest form. Two claims structure it. First, the *guru* is the operative locus of *brahma-vidyā*; the seeker cannot reach *mahāvākya*-cognition without the *guru*'s teaching, since the cognition is not produced by inference but disclosed by instruction. Second, surrender to the *guru* — *bhava śaṅkara deśika me śaraṇam*, the refrain at the close of every verse — is therefore the *paramārtha*-fruit available to the seeker in his current condition. The longer *Śrutisāra-samuddharaṇa* ("Extraction of the Essence of the Vedas"), traditionally ascribed to Toṭaka, is a more discursive verse-treatise summarizing core Advaita doctrine: the four *mahāvākyas*, *adhyāsa*, *avidyā*, and the path of *jñāna*. Its ascription is less firmly fixed than that of the *Aṣṭaka*; some scholars place it later on stylistic grounds. Like Hastāmalaka, the figure of Toṭaka is known principally through tradition and through devotional poetry rather than through systematic doctrinal exposition.

engaged_works.title: Totakashtaka
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Traditionally ascribed; received by the Advaita maṭha tradition.
engaged_works.summary: Eight verses (*aṣṭaka*) in *toṭaka* metre — a tetrameter whose name the disciple is traditionally said to have received from the work itself — addressed in the second person to Śaṅkara. Each verse closes with the refrain *bhava śaṅkara deśika me śaraṇam*: "Be, Śaṅkara my preceptor, my refuge." The verses move through reverence for Śaṅkara as the embodiment of *brahma-vidyā* (verse 1), the disciple's incapacity without the *guru*'s grace (verse 2), Śaṅkara's role as the dispeller of the *saṃsāra*-illusion (verse 3), and the *guru* as the ocean of *kāruṇya*; the sequence culminates in the surrender-formula. The hymn is recited liturgically in Advaita *maṭha* contexts and stands in the standard *guru-paramparā* sequence of devotional recitation. Ascription is traditional; the work is universally received in the Advaita *maṭha* tradition.

engaged_works.title: Shrutisara-Samuddharana
engaged_works.composition_dates_notes: If genuinely Toṭaka's, contemporary with Śaṅkara's mature period; some scholars place it later.
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Traditionally ascribed to Toṭaka by the Advaita maṭha tradition; some scholars dispute the ascription on stylistic and doctrinal grounds, with proposals ranging from a near-contemporary disciple to a later Advaita pedagogue who took Toṭaka's name.

ascription_evidence.description: Received as Toṭaka's by the Advaita maṭha tradition.
Modern scholars (e.g. Pollock, Mahadevan) raise stylistic and chronological doubts.

engaged_works.summary: A substantial verse-*prakaraṇa* — "Extraction of the Essence of the Vedas" — traditionally ascribed to Toṭaka. The work treats, in compact verse-paragraphs, the core doctrinal architecture of Advaita: the four *mahāvākyas* with their *jahad-ajahal-lakṣaṇā* analyses, the *adhyāsa* doctrine of superimposition as the universal pre-philosophical condition, the structure of *avidyā* and its relation to *brahman* and *jīva*, the *jñāna-niṣṭhā* / *karma-niṣṭhā* polarity, and the path of *śravaṇa-manana-nididhyāsana* culminating in *aparokṣa-jñāna*. The work is considerably longer than the *Toṭakāṣṭaka* and serves as a doctrinal complement: it supplies the systematic content that the *Aṣṭaka*'s devotional surrender presupposes. The ascription is accepted within the Advaita *maṭha* lineages but disputed in parts of modern scholarship. Stylistically the verses show some technical formulations more characteristic of post-Sureśvara Advaita than of an immediate Śaṅkara disciple — Pollock and Mahadevan have raised chronological doubts on this basis — leading some to propose a later authorship under the same traditional name. The work is in any case the principal Advaita treatise associated with Toṭaka's lineage-figure in the school's living tradition.
